Washington Orienteers In Japan

Rick Breseman and Ellen Boomer

Due to his frequent business trips to the "Land of the Rising Sun", Rick Breseman has decided to incorporate orienteering into his travel itinerary. Maps in Japan tend to be on steep terrain that has been highly developed on major portions of the map and nearly always includes rice fields — these are mapped as open yellow — though it is permissible to run only on the berms between the fields.

Events tend to be extremely well organized, with registration required far in advance of major events. During a recent trip Rick attended events on Japan's "other coast", in Toyama Prefecture; one event was hosted by the Toyama University Club and the other was the Eastern Japan National Event - one of Japan's "big four". His performance was "ma-ma" (so-so) - barely in the top half for his course. Armed with local terrain knowledge he plans to have fun and improve his standing when attending another of the "big four" events north of Tokyo in Saitama Prefecture toward the end of November.

[Many of you know Ellen Boomer, a regular orienteer for the last several years, well known for a consistently cheerful personality (even when her orienteering is not so consistent). Having finished her studies at the University, she is spending a year in Japan on a Fulbright scholarship, and writes to fill us in on "the meaningless details of my life in Japan." – Ed.]

I am now approaching the end of my first month in Japan. So far, I guess I would have to say things are going better than I expected, however, I am too much of a pessimist to always think so. Gradually, I am making friends, and very slowly and gradually, I am starting to understand little by little of what is being discussed in the college courses I am taking. I am in Hyogo Prefecture (near Kobe and Osaka) attending the Hyogo university of teacher Education. I live in the foreign student dorm on campus, which is very nice, and it only costs me about $33 a month to live there. The Fulbright grant has been overly generous as far as covering expenses. Even though I am in a foreign student dorm, there are only three of us who speak English, most foreigners there are from China, and speak no English whatsoever, so I get to practice Japanese a lot! I am one of 2 Americans who lives in town, so I get stared at a lot and one constant question is always "how tall are you?" (I am 175 cm tall!) In an effort to meet friends, I joined one of the many clubs on campus, the Ping-Pong club. I think I misunderstood the concept of a "club" and thought that it would be very laid back and relaxed aimed at beginners or people with an interest. I was very wrong. Everyone in the Ping-Pong club seems to have had years of playing experience and they closely rival many orienteers I know (I am not saying names!) in their intensity and passion for playing...so basically, in other words, I feel really stupid and I am really bad! But, they are nice and patient to me, so I find myself going to practice several times a week. The one thing that shocks me is despite what appears to be their athletic image (we do warm up stretches and exercises before playing!) almost every single one of them pulls out their cigarettes and starts smoking immediately after practice. I find this almost comical. I am wondering if Japanese orienteers also do the same thing? It would, in a way, be great if they actually smoked during their courses ... it would be so easy to follow the competition in the woods by merely following the trail of smoke and the scent! Speaking of orienteering, I almost went orienteering a few weekends ago, but it turned out to be too far and too expensive to get there. I do have some informa-

tion about an upcoming late November meet nearer to where I live so I am eagerly awaiting it so I can attend. I was very impressed by the Japanese orienteering association. I wrote their Tokyo office a letter requesting meet information and they actually called me up to talk to me. Now if only I could read all the Japanese characters on the information they sent me.....!

If you want to, you can throw my address in the Bearing, I love to get mail! And if people want to be really nice, they can send me cookies and candy! (maybe some of Bruce's brownies!) Here is my address:

By the way, Yashiro is a very beautiful town, small (20,000) but I think despite the inconveniences of shopping for groceries and getting around—no trains, only buses) I like it better here than big cities. The rice fields are very pretty and I am surrounded by rolling green hills!

From The Tales Of Sacajawea

by Elis Eberlein, President

During the last months we have had some meets in Tri-Cities and Walla Walla with not very good participation. However, on the good note is that we had a successful cooperation with EWOC in Spokane to organize the Inland Northwest Cup in September. This kind of cooperation will probably continue during the next couple of years.
This season is not done though. We still have a meet at Horn Rapids County Park on December 2. Just like last year the meet is followed in the afternoon by two very important events. At 2 pm we will have our 2nd Annual Meeting, where will discuss the future of the club, and have election of our club officials. The meeting will again be followed by my selection of a Swedish Christmas Smorgasbord with herring (at least some), meat balls, ham, Ris a la Malta (a dessert), glogg (a hot spicy wine drink), ginger snaps, and so on. These afternoon events will take place at our house in Richland. However, I need to know the number of people who plan to attend, so I can cook an appropriate amount of food. So please let me know by November 29 if you plan to attend. Orienteering visitors from near and far are also welcome. Hjertligt Velkomna!

Belgian Orienteers Visit Us

Will Sperry

Ellensburg OC has taken advantage of the fact that orienteering is an international sport. EOC member Michel Billaux suggested that we become "twins" with his Belgian home club OC Altair, and invite them to the Bullfrog meet and a visit in the Northwest. This idea sounded great and we charged ahead with the plans.
You probably saw our Belgian visitors at the Bullfrog events or the rogaine. Their stay with us in Ellensburg included three days of O', a Yakima River float, and lots of eating and talking. Language was only a slight problem, and we would try our best to understand each other for a while and then fall back exhausted into our own groups to try to determine what had been said. It was great fun.
Eric Hully, the president of the French speaking part of the Belgian O' federation, and I talked about short course O'. Eric organized the European short course championships last year, and we had the US ones here then too. The general formats were the same, two competitions in the same day with a few hours between them; but the courses were quite different. We has 9 or 10 controls in each course on a 1:15000 map while he planned 25 control courses on 1:5000 maps. The courses were about the same total lengths. Eric explained that with all the classes and controls, his competitors often saw several controls and had to be very accurate. They were required to run as fast as they could while continually keep the concentration associated with precision O'. He thought that this produced a unique style of O' with about 180m between controls, whereas our short course format with about 450m between controls looked like a regular O meet divided into two parts. Belgium is hosting the 1997 Junior World Championships and Eric is already busy with the planning.

Field Notes

  • Airfare Update: There is a new fall fare sale which lasts through Friday. There are some very good fares, particularly on TWA. There are also European fares I haven't seen in years, if ever. For example, TWA has a non-weekend, round trip from Seattle to London for $348. Fare has a lot of black-out dates and must be complete by February 29, but that is less than most fares to Chicago. It is too late to get Detroit fares for the US champs (there was a one-day window on Friday for sale fares, but I was out of town). But fares to the east coast (e.g. Georgia in January or North Carolina or Pound Ridge next month) are about the same as the last fare war at the end of August - $350 to $390, with some TWA fares under $300, (and I think Mike still has a $100 off coupon on NW) which would be under $300. Weekend fares may run slightly higher. Bay Area (Long-O is Nov 18/19) still under $100. Remember, fares can, and do, change almost daily. — Bruce McAlister

  • Saturday, 14 October, CROC held an unofficial "B" Meet at Powell Butte in Portland, OR on an absolutely drop-dead gorgeous Autumn day! A record 134 participants enjoyed the updated CAD map and 4 courses designed by Peter Kehoe. Attendance was boosted by a Clinic given on October 4 at the Jantzen Beach REI by Terradan.

  • Anyone wondering why they didn't see Anne York's smiling face at recent summer meets, including the one on Bull Frog maps, she's been traveling. The 2nd week of August saw her down in Albuquerque, N.M. and environs attending a reunion of her Peace Corps group that served in Columbia, and visiting family. She had a 16 hour conjugal visit the evening of the 13th and caught an 0800 flight north on the 14th. North meant through Anchorage where she changed from a jet to a small turbo prop plane to fly in to the Pribilof Islands in the Bering Sea. St. Paul, the larger of the 2 inhabited islands with a year-round community of 400 people, is where Anne usually works when up there; this year she had to go to St. George, population 100. The Pribilofs generally have a combination of fog, wind, or rain which can make landing a plane quite a challenge. This year was no exception. She landed at St. Paul and then had the choice of either flying back to Anchorage or waiting in St. Paul for the next scheduled flight to try again in 2 days. She chose to wait because she be closer to the fascinating northern fur seals there. The weather got no better and the plane again couldn't make it so she caught a ride on a tour boat bringing wildlife lovers to watch birds and seals (and sometimes scientists). Working in rain and 40 mph winds on fur seal rookeries required strength and spirit, not unlike what makes for successful orienteering. — Harvey Friedman

  • Pig War VII, the annual BC-WA orienteering competition was recently held as part of the Ruckles Park and Cle Elum Ridge orienteering events. Much like the real Pig War, the orienteering Pig War is fought for little understood reasons and by rules even less understood. But for the record, B.C. won, although no-one seems to know the actual final score.

  • We were hoping to have some results from the U.S. Champs in this issue, but unfortunately we don't yet know what they were! John Rogers reports that when he left on Sunday afternoon, there was a lot of discussion about what the final results were, particularly because there were many questions as to the accuracy of the calculated times being posted (everyone appeared to have a posted time on Sunday which was 1:20 faster than they recorded on their watch, and it added 100 minutes to some peoples times), and also because the computer program did not compile 2-day results, identify US Champion qualifiers, and did not compile the results by class, only by course. Is it funny how often computerized results create more problems than manually calculated and posted ones?

  • COC and Sammamish have mostly completed their event planning for the next year. This year, they have decided to adopt a system of COC planning events on the first weekend of each month, and Sammamish on the third weekend. Depending on venues, other events, conflicts, and so forth, there will be a number of exceptions, but we're hoping this guideline will results in more regularly spaced events. As always, if there are specific events or locations you would like to see used, especially if you are willing to be meet director or course designer, please contact your club president. Look for a tentative 1996 schedule in the next Bearing 315.

The Bay Area Orienteering Club

invites you to attend
The U. S. Long Course Championships and USOF Two-Day "A" Meet
November 18th & 19th, 1995
at Joseph Grant Park, San Jose, California

Terrain: Joseph Grant Park is an ideal area in which to hold a long course event. The area is predominantly oak forest and grassland with very little undergrowth, which allows for fast running. The mapped area is quite large, about 15 km², and has steep ridge and valley terrain, allowing many route choices. There is a relatively sparse trail network. Wildlife is abundant, including herds of wild pigs. The park, a facility of the Santa Clara County Parks & Recreation Department, is situated in a valley on the road to Mt. Hamilton.

Map: The original five color map was field checked in February, 1984, by Lars Sjokvist and Egil Iversen. Risto Laiho of Finland revised the original map, field checked a new area in December, 1991, and drafted it in April, 1992. The scale is 1:15,000 with a contour interval of 5 meters.

The following table lists which classes will be offered as long courses. In addition, there will be a Two Day "A" Meet held concurrently. Standard length courses of White, Yellow, Orange, Brown, Green, Red, and Blue will be offered both days. All 46 classes will be offered for the regular length 2 Day "A" Meet. There will be staggered starts on all courses, including the long courses.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest November, 1995 [Logo: Cascade Orienteering Club]

Due to his frequent business trips to the "Land of the Rising Sun", Rick Breseman has decided to incorporate orienteering into his travel itinerary. Maps in Japan tend to be on steep terrain that has been highly developed on major portions of the map and nearly always includes rice fields — these are mapped as open yellow — though it is permissible to run only on the berms between the fields.

Events tend to be extremely well organized, with registration required far in advance of major events. During a recent trip Rick attended events on Japan's "other coast", in Toyama Prefecture; one event was hosted by the Toyama University Club and the other was the Eastern Japan National Event - one of Japan's "big four". His performance was "ma-ma" (so-so) - barely in the top half for his course. Armed with local terrain knowledge he plans to have fun and improve his standing when attending another of the "big four" events north of Tokyo in Saitama Prefecture toward the end of November.

Copyright © 1995, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for free distribution for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.

[Many of you know Ellen Boomer, a regular orienteer for the last several years, well known for a consistently cheerful personality (even when her orienteering is not so consistent). Having finished her studies at the University, she is spending a year in Japan on a Fulbright scholarship, and writes to fill us in on "the meaningless details of my life in Japan." – Ed.]

I am now approaching the end of my first month in Japan. So far, I guess I would have to say things are going better than I expected, however, I am too much of a pessimist to always think so. Gradually, I am making friends, and very slowly and gradually, I am starting to understand little by little of what is being discussed in the college courses I am taking. I am in Hyogo Prefecture (near Kobe and Osaka) attending the Hyogo university of teacher Education. I live in the foreign student dorm on campus, which is very nice, and it only costs me about $33 a month to live there. The Fulbright grant has been overly generous as far as covering expenses. Even though I am in a foreign student dorm, there are only three of us who speak English, most foreigners there are from China, and speak no English whatsoever, so I get to practice Japanese a lot! I am one of 2 Americans who lives in town, so I get stared at a lot and one constant question is always "how tall are you?" (I am 175 cm tall!) In an effort to meet friends, I joined one of the many clubs on campus, the Ping-Pong club. I think I misunderstood the concept of a "club" and thought that it would be very laid back and relaxed aimed at beginners or people with an interest. I was very wrong. Everyone in the Ping-Pong club seems to have had years of playing experience and they closely rival many orienteers I know (I am not saying names!) in their intensity and passion for playing...so basically, in other words, I feel really stupid and I am really bad! But, they are nice and patient to me, so I find myself going to practice several times a week. The one thing that shocks me is despite what appears to be their athletic image (we do warm up stretches and exercises before playing!) almost every single one of them pulls out their cigarettes and starts smoking immediately after practice. I find this almost comical. I am wondering if Japanese orienteers also do the same thing? It would, in a way, be great if they actually smoked during their courses ... it would be so easy to follow the competition in the woods by merely following the trail of smoke and the scent! Speaking of orienteering, I almost went orienteering a few weekends ago, but it turned out to be too far and too expensive to get there. I do have some informa-

tion about an upcoming late November meet nearer to where I live so I am eagerly awaiting it so I can attend. I was very impressed by the Japanese orienteering association. I wrote their Tokyo office a letter requesting meet information and they actually called me up to talk to me. Now if only I could read all the Japanese characters on the information they sent me.....!

If you want to, you can throw my address in the Bearing, I love to get mail! And if people want to be really nice, they can send me cookies and candy! (maybe some of Bruce's brownies!) Here is my address:
Ellen Boomer Hyogo University of Teacher Education International House Room 406 942-1 Shimokume Yashiro-cho, Kato-gun Hyogo-ken 673-14 Japan

By the way, Yashiro is a very beautiful town, small (20,000) but I think despite the inconveniences of shopping for groceries and getting around—no trains, only buses) I like it better here than big cities. The rice fields are very pretty and I am surrounded by rolling green hills!

Ellensburg OC has taken advantage of the fact that orienteering is an international sport. EOC member Michel Billaux suggested that we become "twins" with his Belgian home club OC Altair, and invite them to the Bullfrog meet and a visit in the Northwest. This idea sounded great and we charged ahead with the plans. You probably saw our Belgian visitors at the Bullfrog events or the rogaine. Their stay with us in Ellensburg included three days of O', a Yakima River float, and lots of eating and talking. Language was only a slight problem, and we would try our best to understand each other for a while and then fall back exhausted into our own groups to try to determine what had been said. It was great fun. Eric Hully, the president of the French speaking part of the Belgian O' federation, and I talked about short course O'. Eric organized the European short course championships last year, and we had the US ones here then too. The general formats were the same, two competitions in the same day with a few hours between them; but the courses were quite different. We has 9 or 10 controls in each course on a 1:15000 map while he planned 25 control courses on 1:5000 maps. The courses were about the same total lengths. Eric explained that with all the classes and controls, his competitors often saw several controls and had to be very accurate. They were required to run as fast as they could while continually keep the concentration associated with precision O'. He thought that this produced a unique style of O' with about 180m between controls, whereas our short course format with about 450m between controls looked like a regular O meet divided into two parts. Belgium is hosting the 1997 Junior World Championships and Eric is already busy with the planning.

Anyone wondering why they didn't see Anne York's smiling face at recent summer meets, including the one on Bull Frog maps, she's been traveling. The 2nd week of August saw her down in Albuquerque, N.M. and environs attending a reunion of her Peace Corps group that served in Columbia, and visiting family. She had a 16 hour conjugal visit the evening of the 13th and caught an 0800 flight north on the 14th. North meant through Anchorage where she changed from a jet to a small turbo prop plane to fly in to the Pribilof Islands in the Bering Sea. St. Paul, the larger of the 2 inhabited islands with a year-round community of 400 people, is where Anne usually works when up there; this year she had to go to St. George, population 100. The Pribilofs generally have a combination of fog, wind, or rain which can make landing a plane quite a challenge. This year was no exception. She landed at St. Paul and then had the choice of either flying back to Anchorage or waiting in St. Paul for the next scheduled flight to try again in 2 days. She chose to wait because she be closer to the fascinating northern fur seals there. The weather got no better and the plane again couldn't make it so she caught a ride on a tour boat bringing wildlife lovers to watch birds and seals (and sometimes scientists). Working in rain and 40 mph winds on fur seal rookeries required strength and spirit, not unlike what makes for successful orienteering. — Harvey Friedman

John Rogers reports that when he left on Sunday afternoon, there was a lot of discussion about what the final results were, particularly because there were many questions as to the accuracy of the calculated times being posted (everyone appeared to have a posted time on Sunday which was 1:20 faster than they recorded on their watch, and it added 100 minutes to some peoples times), and also because the computer program did not compile 2-day results, identify US Champion qualifiers, and did not compile the results by class, only by course. invites you to attend The U. S. Long Course Championships and USOF Two-Day "A" Meet November 18th & 19th, 1995 at Joseph Grant Park, San Jose, California

Terrain: Joseph Grant Park is an ideal area in which to hold a long course event. The area is predominantly oak forest and grassland with very little undergrowth, which allows for fast running. The mapped area is quite large, about 15 km², and has steep ridge and valley terrain, allowing many route choices. There is a relatively sparse trail network. Wildlife is abundant, including herds of wild pigs. The park, a facility of the Santa Clara County Parks & Recreation Department, is situated in a valley on the road to Mt. Hamilton.

Map: The original five color map was field checked in February, 1984, by Lars Sjokvist and Egil Iversen. Risto Laiho of Finland revised the original map, field checked a new area in December, 1991, and drafted it in April, 1992. The scale is 1:15,000 with a contour interval of 5 meters.

Courses and Classes: The Long Course Championships will be held on Saturday, November 18, and will offer long Brown, Green, Red, and Blue courses. The following table lists which classes will be offered as long courses. In addition, there will be a Two Day "A" Meet held concurrently. Standard length courses of White, Yellow, Orange, Brown, Green, Red, and Blue will be offered both days. All 46 classes will be offered for the regular length 2 Day "A" Meet. There will be staggered starts on all courses, including the long courses.

Awards: Awards will be given to the top 3 participants in each class in the Long Course and also for the 2 Day "A" Meet. Participants will be eligible for awards in either the Long Course or the 2 Day "A" Meet, but not both.
Park entrance fee: $3/car/day, or $8/campsite/night. Pay at park.

Pre-Registration: Use the USOF Standard Entry Form. On Line 5 for the Day 1 Course, indicate that you are registering for the Long Course by writing Long in front of the Course Color. Otherwise, we will assume that you wish to register for the standard length course. Mail to the registrar, Dennis Wildfogel, with the entry fee payable to "BAOC" in U.S. funds. (Foreign entrants may pay upon arrival.)
Recreational Courses: White and Yellow recreational courses will be offered. Map hikers need not pre-register. Sign up on site; the usual local BAOC event fees, $6/individual, $8/group, will apply.
Saturday, November 4. Last day to postmark registration to avoid late fee.

Camping: Camping is available at Joseph Grant Park for $8.00 per night. There are 22 campsites available on a first come, first served basis. The park does not accept reservations for camp sites. Hot showers are available. Each campsite may have up to 8 people and 2 cars.

Directions: Joseph Grant Park is about a 40 minute drive from the San Jose Airport. Take the Alum Rock Road exit (Route 130) from either U.S. Highway 101 or Interstate 680, and head east. Turn right on Mt. Hamilton Road, and follow the road up the mountain for 8 miles to the park.
Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
